issues.description: Cholesterol described only as 'keeping the membrane fluid at body temperature' is an oversimplification. Cholesterol is a bidirectional fluidity buffer: at higher temperatures it restrains phospholipid motion (decreases fluidity), and at lower temperatures it prevents tight packing and solidification (maintains fluidity). The current wording captures only the cold-side function. AP-acceptable but technically incomplete.

Phrasing 'the inside slightly negative' attributed to the Na/K ATPase is partially misleading. The Na/K pump is electrogenic and contributes only a small amount (a few mV) to the resting membrane potential; the dominant source of the negative interior is K+ leak channels and the resulting K+ equilibrium potential. Acceptable as a simplification for AP, but a careful reader could infer the pump alone sets RMP.

Listing aquaporins as the canonical example of a 'hydrophilic tunnel' channel is correct, but pairing it directly with 'ion channels for sodium and potassium' as facilitated diffusion can blur a nuance: many physiologically important Na+/K+ channels are gated (voltage- or ligand-gated) rather than always-open facilitated-diffusion channels. Not wrong (gated channels still mediate passive flow when open), but worth a one-line clarification at AP level.

Claim that Na/K ATPase activity burns 'around twenty percent of your resting energy budget' is a whole-body average that ranges roughly 19-28% in the literature and is heavily tissue-dependent (much higher in brain/kidney, lower in resting muscle). The number is defensible as a teaching figure; flagging only because the script states it as a single point estimate without qualification.

'All of this uses ATP' for endocytosis/exocytosis is correct in spirit (vesicle budding, dynamin, SNARE-mediated fusion, and actin remodeling all consume ATP/GTP) but slightly imprecise — much of the direct chemical energy in vesicle fission is GTP hydrolysis by dynamin, and clathrin uncoating uses ATP via Hsc70. For AP this is fine; technically 'ATP/GTP' would be more accurate.

summary: Content is factually sound for AP Biology; only minor oversimplifications in cholesterol function, Na/K pump's contribution to RMP, channel types, and bulk-transport energetics.
